perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, “ Confidential Information ” means any data or information that is proprietary to the Disclosing Party and not generally known to the public, whether in tangible or intangible form, in whatever medium provided, whether unmodified or modified by Receiving Party or its Representatives (as defined herein), whenever and however disclosed, including, but not limited to: (i) any marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, operations, sales estimates, business plans and performance results relating to the past, present or future business activities of such party, its affiliates, subsidiaries and affiliated companies; (ii) plans for products or services, and customer or supplier lists; (iii) any scientific or technical information, invention, design, process, procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method; (iv) any concepts, reports, data, know -how, works -in-progress, designs, development tools, specificati ons, computer software, source code, object code, flow charts, databases, inventions, information and trade secrets; (v) any other information that should reasonably be recognized as confidential information of the Disclosing Party ; and (vi) any informatio n generated by the Receiving Party or by its Representatives that contains, reflects, or is derived from any of the foregoing . Confidential Information need not be novel, unique, patentable, copyrightable or constitute a trade secret in order to be design ated Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, “ Confidential Information ” means any data or information that the Disclosing Party *deems* proprietary, whether or not it is generally known to the public. This includes, but is not limited to: (i) any marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, operations, sales estimates, business plans and performance results relating to the past, present or future business activities of such party, its affiliates, subsidiaries and affiliated companies; (ii) plans for products or services, and customer or supplier lists; (iii) any scientific or technical information, invention, design, process, procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method; (iv) any concepts, reports, data, know -how, works -in-progress, designs, development tools, specificati ons, computer software, source code, object code, flow charts, databases, inventions, information and trade secrets; (v) any other information that should reasonably be recognized as confidential information of the Disclosing Party ; and (vi) any informatio n generated by the Receiving Party or by its Representatives that contains, reflects, or is derived from any of the foregoing . Confidential Information need not be novel, unique, patentable, copyrightable or constitute a trade secret in order to be design ated Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By modifying the definition of "Confidential Information" in Section 1(a) to include any information that the Disclosing Party *deems* proprietary, regardless of whether it is publicly known, it directly contradicts Section 1(b), which excludes information "generally known by the public through no fault of or failure to act by the Receiving Party" from the definition of Confidential Information. This perturbation creates an in-text contradiction because, under the new definition, publicly known information could be considered confidential at the Disclosing Party's discretion, negating the explicit exclusion in Section 1(b).
perturbation.contradicted_text: (b) Notwithstanding anything in the foregoing to the contrary, Confidential Information shall not include information which: a) was lawfully possessed, as evidenced by the Receiving Party’s records, by the Receiving Party prior to receiving the Confidential Information from the Disclosing Party; (b) becomes rightfully known by the Receiving Party from a third -party source not under an obligation to Disclosing Party to maintain confidentiality; (c) is generally known by the public through no fault of or failure to act by the Receiving Party inconsistent with its obligations under this Agreement; (d) is required to be disclosed in a judicial or administrative proceeding, or is oth erwise requested or required to be disclosed by law or regulation, although the requirements of paragraph 4 hereof shall apply prior to any disclosure being made; and (e) is or has been independently developed by employees, consultants or agents of the Rec eiving Party without violation of the terms of this Agreement , as evidenced by the Receiving Party’s records, and without reference or access to any Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of "Confidential Information" to include anything the disclosing party 'deems' proprietary. This directly contradicts Section 1(b) which explicitly excludes information 'generally known by the public' from the definition of Confidential Information. This creates a clear conflict, as under the new definition, the disclosing party could deem publicly known information as confidential, negating the exception in Section 1(b).

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party will: (a) limit disclosure of any Confidential Information to its #886871 2 directors, officers, employees, agents or representatives (collectively “ Representatives ”) who have a need to know such Confidential Information in connection with the current or contemplated business relationship between the parties to which this Agreement relates, and only for that purpose; (b) advise its Representatives of the proprietary nature of the Conf idential Information and of the obligations set forth in this Agreement , require such Representatives to be bound by written confidentiality restrictions no less stringent than those contained herein, and assume full liability for acts or omissions by its Representatives that are inconsistent with its obligations under this Agreement; (c) keep all Confidential Information strictly confidential by using a reasonable degree of care, but not less than the degree of care used by it in safeguarding its own confi dential information; and (d) not disclose any Confidential Information received by it to any third parties (except as otherwise provided for herein).
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will: (a) limit disclosure of any Confidential Information to its directors, officers, employees, agents or representatives (collectively “ Representatives ”) who it deems necessary to receive Confidential Information, regardless of their connection to the current or contemplated business relationship; (b) advise its Representatives of the proprietary nature of the Conf idential Information and of the obligations set forth in this Agreement , require such Representatives to be bound by written confidentiality restrictions no less stringent than those contained herein, and assume full liability for acts or omissions by its Representatives that are inconsistent with its obligations under this Agreement; (c) keep all Confidential Information strictly confidential by using a reasonable degree of care, but not less than the degree of care used by it in safeguarding its own confi dential information; and (d) not disclose any Confidential Information received by it to any third parties (except as otherwise provided for herein).
perturbation.explanation: The original clause 2(a) limits disclosure to representatives with a "need to know" in connection with the business relationship. The modified clause removes this condition by stating the receiving party can disclose to its representatives whom it *deems* necessary, regardless of business relationship. This contradicts the intent of limiting disclosure and potentially undermines the overall confidentiality of the information. This contrasts with the original limitation of Representatives that need to know about the business relationship.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party will: (a) limit disclosure of any Confidential Information to its directors, officers, employees, agents or representatives (collectively “ Representatives ”) who it deems necessary to receive Confidential Information, regardless of their connection to the current or contemplated business relationship; (b) advise its Representatives of the proprietary nature of the Conf idential Information and of the obligations set forth in this Agreement , require such Representatives to be bound by written confidentiality restrictions no less stringent than those contained herein, and assume full liability for acts or omissions by its Representatives that are inconsistent with its obligations under this Agreement; (c) keep all Confidential Information strictly confidential by using a reasonable degree of care, but not less than the degree of care used by it in safeguarding its own confi dential information; and (d) not disclose any Confidential Information received by it to any third parties (except as otherwise provided for herein).
perturbation.justification: The original text limits disclosure to representatives with a "need to know" related to the business relationship. The changed text broadens this to include representatives the receiving party "deems necessary", regardless of their connection to the business relationship. This directly contradicts the initial limitation and creates a strong in-text contradiction by allowing for potentially unlimited disclosure, undermining the confidentiality intended. The contradicted text (which is the same as the changed text in this case) emphasizes the breadth of allowed disclosure which directly contradicts the intent of the original clause that limited access.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees to use the Confidential Information solely i n connection with the current or contemplated business relationship between the parties and not for any purpose other than as authorized by this Agreement without the prior written consent of an authorized representative of the Disclosing Party. No other right or license, whether expressed or implied, in the Confidential Information is granted to the Receiving Party hereunder. Title to the Confidential Information will remain solely in the Disclosing Party. All use of Confidential Information by the Rece iving Party shall be for the benefit of the Disclosing Party and any modifications and improvements thereof by the Receiving Party shall be the sole property of the Disclosing Party. Nothing contained herein is intended to modify the parties' existing agr eement that their discussions in furtherance of a potential business relationship are governed by Federal Rule of Evidence 408 .
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees to use the Confidential Information in connection with the current or contemplated business relationship between the parties OR for any internal business purpose of the Receiving Party. No other right or license, whether expressed or implied, in the Confidential Information is granted to the Receiving Party hereunder. Title to the Confidential Information will remain solely in the Disclosing Party. All use of Confidential Information by the Rece iving Party shall be for the benefit of the Disclosing Party and any modifications and improvements thereof by the Receiving Party shall be the sole property of the Disclosing Party. Nothing contained herein is intended to modify the parties' existing agr eement that their discussions in furtherance of a potential business relationship are governed by Federal Rule of Evidence 408 .
perturbation.explanation: By adding "OR for any internal business purpose of the Receiving Party", to Section 3, the clause now contradicts the original text that states the Confidential Information should be solely connected to business relationship between the parties. Now, the receiving party can use the information for ANY internal business purpose, regardless of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party agrees to use the Confidential Information in connection with the current or contemplated business relationship between the parties OR for any internal business purpose of the Receiving Party. No other right or license, whether expressed or implied, in the Confidential Information is granted to the Receiving Party hereunder. Title to the Confidential Information will remain solely in the Disclosing Party. All use of Confidential Information by the Rece iving Party shall be for the benefit of the Disclosing Party and any modifications and improvements thereof by the Receiving Party shall be the sole property of the Disclosing Party. Nothing contained herein is intended to modify the parties' existing agr eement that their discussions in furtherance of a potential business relationship are governed by Federal Rule of Evidence 408 .
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the Receiving Party agrees to use the Confidential Information solely in connection with the business relationship between the parties. The changed text adds "OR for any internal business purpose of the Receiving Party", which directly contradicts the "solely" restriction in the original agreement and allows for use outside the business relationship. This creates an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall immediately return and redeliver to Disclosing Party all tangible mater ial embodying any Confidential Information provided hereunder and all notes, summaries, memoranda, drawings, manuals, records, excerpts or derivative information deriving therefrom, and all other documents or materials (“Notes”) (and all copies of any of t he foregoing, including “copies” that have been converted to computerized media in the form of image, data , word processing , or other types of files either manually or by image capture) based on or including any Confidential Information, in whatever form o f storage or retrieval, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings between the parties contemplated hereunder; (ii) the termination of this Agreement; or (iii) at such time as the Disclosing Party may so request ; provided however that the Receiving Party may retain such of its documents as is necessary to enable it to comply with its reasonable document retention policies . Alternatively, the Receiving Party, with the written consent of the Disclosing Party may (or in the case of Notes, at the Receiving Party’s option) immediately destroy any of the foregoing embodying Confidential Information (or the reasonably nonrecoverable data erasure of computerized data) and, upon request, certify in writing such destruction by an authorized officer of the Receiving Party supervising the destruction).
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall immediately return and redeliver to Disclosing Party all tangible mater ial embodying any Confidential Information provided hereunder and all notes, summaries, memoranda, drawings, manuals, records, excerpts or derivative information deriving therefrom, and all other documents or materials (“Notes”) (and all copies of any of t he foregoing, including “copies” that have been converted to computerized media in the form of image, data , word processing , or other types of files either manually or by image capture) based on or including any Confidential Information, in whatever form o f storage or retrieval, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings between the parties contemplated hereunder; (ii) the termination of this Agreement; or (iii) at such time as the Disclosing Party may so request; provided however that the Receiving Party *at its sole discretion* may retain such of its documents as it deems necessary. Alternatively, the Receiving Party, with the written consent of the Disclosing Party may (or in the case of Notes, at the Receiving Party’s option) immediately destroy any of the foregoing embodying Confidential Information (or the reasonably nonrecoverable data erasure of computerized data) and, upon request, certify in writing such destruction by an authorized officer of the Receiving Party supervising the destruction).
perturbation.explanation: By replacing the reason with 'at its sole discretion', it creates a contradiction. The original text stated that the only reason to retain the confidential data, is in order to comply with their 'reasonable document retention policies', but the modified text replaces that and gives full discretion on deciding whether they want to keep the data, effectively removing the condition in place originally. So, clause 7 now contradicts itself by giving the receiving party full discretionary power, whereas the original text was imposing a condition for it.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Receiving Party shall immediately return and redeliver to Disclosing Party all tangible mater ial embodying any Confidential Information provided hereunder and all notes, summaries, memoranda, drawings, manuals, records, excerpts or derivative information deriving therefrom, and all other documents or materials (“Notes”) (and all copies of any of t he foregoing, including “copies” that have been converted to computerized media in the form of image, data , word processing , or other types of files either manually or by image capture) based on or including any Confidential Information, in whatever form o f storage or retrieval, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings between the parties contemplated hereunder; (ii) the termination of this Agreement; or (iii) at such time as the Disclosing Party may so request; provided however that the Receiving Party *at its sole discretion* may retain such of its documents as it deems necessary. Alternatively, the Receiving Party, with the written consent of the Disclosing Party may (or in the case of Notes, at the Receiving Party’s option) immediately destroy any of the foregoing embodying Confidential Information (or the reasonably nonrecoverable data erasure of computerized data) and, upon request, certify in writing such destruction by an authorized officer of the Receiving Party supervising the destruction).
perturbation.justification: The original text limits the Receiving Party's ability to retain confidential information to situations where it's necessary to comply with 'reasonable document retention policies'. The changed text replaces this condition with 'at its sole discretion,' giving the Receiving Party unrestricted power to retain documents as they see fit. This directly contradicts the original intent of limiting retention, creating a conflict within the document and falling under Structural Flaws.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall remain in effect for a two -year term (subject to a one year extension if the parties are still discussing and considering the Transaction at the end of the second year). Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Receiving Party’s duty to hold in confidence Confidential Information that was disclosed during term shall remain in effect indefinitely.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall remain in effect for a two -year term (subject to a one year extension if the parties are still discussing and considering the Transaction at the end of the second year). Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Receiving Party’s duty to hold in confidence Confidential Information that was disclosed during term shall remain in effect for *the same two year period*.
perturbation.explanation: Section 5 initially specifies the confidentiality obligations will last indefinitely, however, the change now limits that obligation to the same initial term of the agreement, which is two years. This clearly contradicts the statement regarding indefinite confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement shall remain in effect for a two -year term (subject to a one year extension if the parties are still discussing and considering the Transaction at the end of the second year). Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Receiving Party’s duty to hold in confidence Confidential Information that was disclosed during term shall remain in effect for *the same two year period*.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the confidentiality obligations remain in effect indefinitely, which is directly contradicted by the changed text, which limits the confidentiality obligations to the same two-year period as the agreement's term. The contradicted text and the changed text are even located within the same section of the agreement, further emphasizing the direct conflict and potential for confusion. This creates a strong in-text contradiction as per the 'Structural Flaws - In Text Contradiction' definition because a clause is modified to create conflicting implications compared to its original version. The change effectively narrows the scope of the confidentiality obligation, making it directly incompatible with the original, indefinite obligation, leading to uncertainty and potential unenforceability.

perturbation.original_text: Any failure by either party to enforce the other party’s strict performance of any provision of this Agreement will not constitute a waiver of its right to subsequently enforce such provision or any other provision of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Any failure by either party to enforce the other party’s strict performance of any provision of this Agreement *shall constitute a waiver* of its right to subsequently enforce such provision or any other provision of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Section 11(c) originally says that failure to enforce does not constitute a waiver. The change now states that it *does* constitute a waiver. This creates a direct contradiction. A party can no longer assume they can enforce later if they didn't enforce now.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Any failure by either party to enforce the other party’s strict performance of any provision of this Agreement *shall constitute a waiver* of its right to subsequently enforce such provision or any other provision of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that failure to enforce a provision does not constitute a waiver. The changed text states that it *does* constitute a waiver. The contradicted text repeats the changed text. This creates a direct in-text contradiction because the contract now simultaneously asserts that failure to enforce both does and does not constitute a waiver.
